HMW-V01: Responsive Job Table

How might we adapt the vendor's job list table to work on 768px tablet screens without losing the scanability that makes the dense table design effective?
Context: Vendor operators are often on-site at warehouses and cargo terminals, using tablets or smaller laptops. The admin app requires 1200px minimum. The vendor app commits to 768px+ from day 1. At 768px, a 7-column table doesn't fit. We need a strategy that preserves the "smart spreadsheet" feel while actually being usable on narrower screens.

Option A: Horizontal Scroll Table

Simplest
Keep the exact same table at all widths. Wrap it in a horizontal scroll container. The table doesn't change — the viewport just shows a window into it. Users swipe right to see more columns.
Pros
• Zero design work — same table everywhere
• Familiar pattern (spreadsheet users scroll constantly)
• No information loss, every column visible by scrolling
Cons
• Status and Cost hidden off-screen (the two most important columns for action/reconciliation)
• Can't see the full row at once — defeats scanability
• Feels unfinished, not designed for the screen
768px tablet
Teleport OS Vendor My Jobs HaleSun
18 jobs · 4 pending · 6 in progress
Active 10 Completed 3 Verified 5
Shipment Customer Service Route Pickup Status Cost
DO-2603-001 TikTok Global EC Baoan WH, Shenzhen → SZX Cargo Terminal 29 Mar
08:00		 Pending CNY 2,800
DO-2603-003 Shopee SG FM Nanshan Hub → Futian Checkpoint → HK Airport 29 Mar
10:30		 In Progress CNY 3,450
DO-2603-005 Shein CN EC Panyu WH, Guangzhou → CAN Airport Terminal 28 Mar
14:00		 Completed CNY 4,100
← scroll to see Status and Cost →

Option B: Condensed Table (Drop + Stack Columns)

Recommended
Redesign the table for narrow screens: merge Customer + Shipment into one stacked cell, drop Route entirely (it's in the detail page), and keep Status + Cost always visible. The key insight: at 768px, the vendor needs to decide which job to open, not read the full route. Customer, Service, Status, and Cost are the decision columns.
Pros
• Status and Cost always visible — the two action/reconciliation columns
• No horizontal scroll needed
• Still feels like a table, not a mobile redesign
• Route available one tap away in detail page
Cons
• Route not visible in list (vendor can't scan "where do I need to go" from the list)
• Stacked cells look denser, slightly harder to scan vertically
• Two different table layouts to maintain (wide vs narrow)
768px tablet — condensed columns
Teleport OS Vendor My Jobs HaleSun

09:00		 Verified CNY 2,650
Temu
DO-2603-008
 OH 29 Mar
11:00		 Pending CNY 1,900

Option C: Card List (Mobile Pattern)

Radical
Abandon the table entirely at 768px. Each job becomes a card with all key info stacked vertically. This is the standard responsive pattern — tables become cards on small screens.
Pros
• Every field visible without scrolling or truncation
• Tap target is the whole card (easy on touch)
• Natural pattern for mobile/tablet — users expect it
Cons
• Breaks the "smart spreadsheet" design language — feels like a different app
• Much less data density — only ~4 jobs visible at a time vs ~8+ in a table
• Harder to scan and compare across jobs (eyes jump between cards)
• DESIGN.md explicitly says "tables, not cards"
768px tablet — card list

28 Mar · 14:00

CNY 4,100

Recommendation: Option B — Condensed Table

Option B keeps the table as a table — maintaining the platform's design language — while making a smart trade: drop Route (available in detail), keep Status and Cost visible (the decision/reconciliation columns). The vendor uses the list to decide "which job do I open next?" and that decision is driven by Status + Cost + Customer, not Route. Stacking Customer/Shipment into one cell is a clean density trick that buys back the space.

Option A (scroll) is lazy — hiding Status and Cost off-screen defeats the purpose of the table. Option C (cards) breaks the design system and halves data density. Option B is the Goldilocks: still a table, still dense, but fits 768px without compromise.
